text: The descriptions of fluids and processes may seem repulsive or alien, or simply funny or strange. They’re not what we normally encounter as our bodies. This is the deal that science strikes with us. It will tell us, unblinkingly, what is there and what is “real,” but in exchange, we must accept this as the truth, whether we experience it as true or not. We shouldn’t dismiss what science has to tell us, but what if we didn’t have to trade experience for information? A description of the body that approaches our everyday encounter with it, without the scientific description, is something like this: the body is a location for experienced feeling and meaningful sensations. It’s a feeling of hunger or tiredness. It can also be the opposite—being satiated or energetic. It’s a feeling of horniness and strength. When we consider our bodies in our everyday lives, when we’re really connected to them, it’s always through a feeling. Something in our body lights up in our experience. Not distant but scientific concepts such as bile production, but rather as, “I’m hungry.” 1 The phenomenon that can give us the best insight into this is the sense of touch. In his extraordinary book, Our Twelve Senses , Albert Soesman (taking cues from Austrian scientist, philosopher, and mystic Rudolf Steiner) explores touch from a phenomenological perspective. What do we really experience through touch? First, he asks us to imagine what life would be like if we possessed only our sense of touch: With sense of touch I do not feel whether something is warm or cold, whether it is rough or smooth. I do not notice my balance changing. What is it I actually become aware of, then, when I use touch only, leaving everything else out?… I beg your pardon, but would you please try to be a naked worm?… [A] worm in the pitch dark. What would it feel like when for the first time you experienced touch?… You see, don’t you, if you had no senses at all, and for the first time you had this sense of touch, and you “wormed” up against this pebble, you would experience something: “It” … namely an awareness of boundary . 2 In other words, all we experience through touch where we end and where what isn’t us begins. The “it” that Soesman writes about is a sense of boundary and separation. Maybe as you read this, you’ve been touching the chair you’re sitting in or you’re leaning against your lover. Feel. There is you, and there is not-you. Our bodies, through touch, provide us with our individual boundary. This is something we experience without needing to conceptualize it. It’s not like explaining stomach acid, which can be explained and explained, but never sensed in the same way. This is the description of sensation; what is more revealing is the inner truth this sensation gives us. Soesman goes on: There is a peculiar paradox in the sense of touch: on the one hand you can feel that a piece of wood, for example, is something hard, but on the other hand you can feel that it is a fine piece of wood. You can feel: How beautiful it is, doesn’t it feel good when you run your hand over it. This is the peculiar thing: In the hardness we experience being excluded from it, and in the pleasure of touching we experience the desire to make an intimate connection. In this we see an innate desire of the human being; he actually knows that the sense of touch separates him, excludes him from something, to which he is yet related. We are totally closed off from the cosmos … and we end up snuggling up against it, as it were. 3 We touch one another—whether this touch is a caress or a hug or oral sex—it’s how we enact intimacy. And just as we’re all bound up in these bodies, and every time we touch something, we experience our own outline and boundary, so are we all connected. I am separate, as are you. Here’s my boundary, here’s yours, so we have a union with each other—we share in this experience of bodily separation. Each time I touch you, it’s comforting, because this sharing of separation is what makes us (and reveals us to be) not separate. So we see that the outer experience reveals its opposite, which is an inner truth . Science alone can’t quite come to terms with this. In Euclidian geometry, parallel lines never meet. But in the world of meaning, they always meet. They don’t meet in the way perpendicular lines do. Instead, their common point is their form . They meet conceptually because they share the same form. Again, the outer truth reveals an inner truth through its opposite . All this means that material presents itself to us not merely as outside-of-us matter and process, but also as inner experience. In esoteric traditions, the human being is composed of four bodies. The word body may be confusing here, because we’re used to thinking of it as representing a specific material entity. That’s included in the esoteric conception, but added to. Body also means the different aspects of being that evince different universal laws. 4 There’s the body that science tells us we have—the body made of stuff . It’s flesh and bone—basically water- and stone-like substances. This is the mineral body (what I will refer to throughout as the object-body ) of esoteric literature. But of course the body doesn’t change like a river or a stone does—it isn’t merely added to from or changed by forces outside itself. It grows from within . It has a different principle governing life and growth. This growth-body is the etheric body . We also have a body that shows up as the meaning-filled emotional responses.
